From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

2007 film by James Wan

This article is about the 2007 film. For other topics, see [Dead Silence (disambiguation)](/wiki/Dead_Silence_(disambiguation) "Dead Silence (disambiguation)").

| Dead Silence | |
| --- | --- |

| Starring | * [Ryan Kwanten](/wiki/Ryan_Kwanten "Ryan Kwanten") * [Amber Valletta](/wiki/Amber_Valletta "Amber Valletta") * [Donnie Wahlberg](/wiki/Donnie_Wahlberg "Donnie Wahlberg") * [Bob Gunton](/wiki/Bob_Gunton "Bob Gunton") |

***Dead Silence*** is a 2007 American [supernatural horror film](/wiki/Supernatural_horror_film "Supernatural horror film") directed by [James Wan](/wiki/James_Wan "James Wan") and written by [Leigh Whannell](/wiki/Leigh_Whannell "Leigh Whannell"). The film stars [Ryan Kwanten](/wiki/Ryan_Kwanten "Ryan Kwanten") as Jamie Ashen, a young widower returning to his hometown to search for answers to his wife's death. It also stars [Amber Valletta](/wiki/Amber_Valletta "Amber Valletta"), [Donnie Wahlberg](/wiki/Donnie_Wahlberg "Donnie Wahlberg"), and [Bob Gunton](/wiki/Bob_Gunton "Bob Gunton").

*Dead Silence* was theatrically released in the United States on March 16, 2007, by [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"). The film was dedicated to [Gregg Hoffman](/wiki/Gregg_Hoffman "Gregg Hoffman"), who died in 2005. Upon release, it was considered a critical and [commercial flop](/wiki/Box-office_bomb "Box-office bomb"), earning $22.4 million on a $20 million production budget, and received negative reviews from critics, who criticized the script, characters and [twist ending](/wiki/Plot_twist "Plot twist"). However, it has since developed a [cult following](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film").

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

Jamie Ashen and his wife Lisa receive an anonymous gift: a [ventriloquist](/wiki/Ventriloquist "Ventriloquist") doll called "Billy". When Jamie goes out, a figure kills Lisa. Jamie returns to find his wife dead with her tongue cut off. He is arrested and interrogated by police detective Jim Lipton, but later released due to a lack of evidence. Inside Billy's box, Jamie finds a mysterious message about "Mary Shaw", a deceased ventriloquist from his hometown, Raven's Fair.

Returning to Raven's Fair, now old and rundown, Jamie visits his estranged, disabled father Edward and his much-younger wife, Ella. He arranges for Lisa's funeral with the help of local mortician Henry Walker. Henry's senile wife, Marion, urges him to bury Billy. Jamie does so but is confronted by Lipton, who finds his actions suspicious.

Henry explains that Mary Shaw was publicly humiliated by a young boy named Michael, who rudely claimed to see her lips moving during one of her performances. Later, after Michael disappeared, his family blamed it on Mary and [lynched](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") her. Mary's last wish was to have her body turned into a doll and buried with her collection of dolls - her "children." Henry, then a child, saw Mary (after she was turned into a dummy) rise up, but was spared thanks to his silence, because Mary only kills those who scream when exposed to her true appearance. Jamie finds out that Michael, who actually *was* murdered by Mary, was his great-uncle. The Ashen family forced Mary to scream and silenced her by cutting her tongue out; she has since been seeking revenge against their bloodline by killing them the same way.

Mary kills Henry, and Lipton discovers that all of her dolls have been dug up. At Mary's old theater, Jamie and Lipton discover 100 of the dolls in their display case, along with Michael's body, which had been turned into a [marionette](/wiki/Marionette "Marionette"). Mary's ghost reveals that she killed Lisa because she was pregnant with his child, thereby killing any potential newborn of the Ashen family. Jamie and Lipton start a fire to destroy the dolls, but Lipton trips and screams, sealing his fate. Back at his father's residence, Jamie is confronted by Mary, but repels her by throwing Billy into the fireplace. He learns, much to his horror, that his father is already dead; the current "Edward" he has been interacting with all this time is a doll converted from his corpse; his voice was provided by Ella, the "perfect doll" that Mary created before her death. Jamie screams as Ella becomes possessed by Mary; he suffers the same fate as everyone else.

Mary flips through a photo album of her victims: Jamie, Lisa, Lipton, Henry and Edward, and finally Ella. She closes the book, having completed her revenge.

## Cast

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

Filming took place in [Toronto, Ontario, Canada](/wiki/Toronto,_Ontario,_Canada "Toronto, Ontario, Canada") in October 2005 and concluded May 2006 [[2]](#cite_note-2) The setting for the film's story, the non-fictional suburb of "Raven's Fair," is situated at Badley Bridge and goes by the name "Raven's Fair Town Sign".[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Release")]

*Dead Silence* was released in the United States on March 16, 2007.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reception")]

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Box office")]

In the United States, as of April 16, 2007, the film's total domestic [gross](/wiki/Gross_(economics) "Gross (economics)") has been worth [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")16.8 million (according to [Box Office Mojo](/wiki/Box_Office_Mojo "Box Office Mojo")), and screenings of *Dead Silence* were ceased in most theatres 16 days following its release; the film's estimated [production budget](/wiki/Production_budget "Production budget") was US$20 million. As of April 1, 2009, US$5,572,971 has been generated globally. Worldwide, the film has grossed $22,382,047.[[1]](#cite_note-BOM-1)

### Critical reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Critical reception")]

On [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), the film has a rating of 21%, based on 84 reviews, with an average rating of 3.90/10. The site's critical consensus reads, "More tasteful than recent slasher flicks, but *Dead Silence* is undone by boring characters, bland dialogue, and an unnecessary and obvious [twist ending](/wiki/Twist_ending "Twist ending")."[[5]](#cite_note-5) On [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), the film holds a score of 34 out of 100, based on 15 reviews, indicating "generally unfavorable" reviews.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "C+" on an A+ to F scale.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Nicholas Barber of *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* gave a positive review, describing the film as "a ludicrous ghost story from the Saw team", while stating: "Dead Silence is a guilty pleasure, especially if you have a gottle of geer beforehand."[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In a negative review, [Peter Bradshaw](/wiki/Peter_Bradshaw "Peter Bradshaw") of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* gave the film a 2 stars out of 5 and wrote: "The first horrifying death that the dummy causes is genuinely pretty scary, though from then on, the story deflates a little, returning to its former power only with the final revelation about Jamie's disabled father, who holds the key to these terrible deaths. A reasonable shocker."[[9]](#cite_note-9) Charles Kassady Jr. of *[Common Sense Media](/wiki/Common_Sense_Media "Common Sense Media")* gave the same rating and wrote: "Some of the scary stuff is just fleeting glimpses of Mary's ghost, reflected in a mirror or in deep shadow. *Dead Silence* actually gets less frightening when the filmmakers apply the fancy CGI special-effects or reveal Mary Shaw in full. It's far more ominous just to show the dummy's staring eyes or grinning face suddenly turned in a different position than the last shot."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Despite the initial negative reception, the film garnered a cult following. Cody Parish of *[PopMatters](/wiki/PopMatters "PopMatters")* said it's "unlike anything Wan has made."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Home video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Home video")]

The film was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [HD DVD](/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD") on June 26, 2007, with an "[unrated](/wiki/Unrated_films "Unrated films")" version also released. The film has since grossed US$17,304,718 in overall DVD sales.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

*Dead Silence* was released on [Blu-ray Disc](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") internationally in 2010 and wasn't released on Blu-ray in the United States until 2015, when it was announced on May 15 that [Universal Studios](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") would be releasing the film to Blu-ray Disc in the U.S. It was released on August 11.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Soundtrack

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Soundtrack")]

| 1. "[We Sleep Forever](/wiki/We_Sleep_Forever "We Sleep Forever")" Released: March 2007 ([Rain in Hell](/wiki/Rain_in_Hell "Rain in Hell")) | |
|  | |

[Lakeshore Records](/wiki/Lakeshore_Records "Lakeshore Records") released the soundtrack of *Dead Silence* on March 20, 2007. The CD contains 31 tracks, the first track being the song "[We Sleep Forever](/wiki/We_Sleep_Forever "We Sleep Forever")" performed by American rock band [Aiden](/wiki/Aiden "Aiden") (despite not actually being featured in the film itself). The rest of the CD is taken up by [Charlie Clouser](/wiki/Charlie_Clouser "Charlie Clouser")'s film score. Clouser previously scored Wan's first commercially released film, *[Saw](/wiki/Saw "Saw")* and its many sequels.

## Post-release commentary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Dead_Silence&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Post-release commentary")]

In his personal [blog](/wiki/Blog "Blog"), screenwriter [Leigh Whannell](/wiki/Leigh_Whannell "Leigh Whannell") reveals the origins of the film within the context of the [Hollywood film industry](/wiki/Film_industry#Hollywood "Film industry"). In a candid post entitled "Dud Silence: The Hellish Experience of Making a Bad Horror Film", Whannell explains that the film was conceived following the advice of his [agent](/wiki/Talent_agent "Talent agent") at the time and that a "[script doctor](/wiki/Script_doctor "Script doctor")" was eventually employed by the [production studio](/wiki/Film_studio "Film studio"). Whannell concludes the post with a description of the key lessons that were learned following the *Dead Silence* experience:

> After everything is said and done, I'm almost glad *Dead Silence* happened, because it gave me an extreme, coal-face lesson in what not to do. It was like learning to swim by leaping off Niagara Falls. I only write scripts on spec now, which means that I write them in my own time without getting paid and then take them out into the world to see if anyone's interested. Never again will I enter the arranged marriage of selling a [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(filmmaking) "Pitch (filmmaking)"). I have also become very gun-shy about working with studios. In the world of independent film, what you write ends up on screen. Plus, they don't have the money to bring in script doctors! Works fine for me. Who knows, maybe one day I will work with a studio again...[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In retrospect, director [James Wan](/wiki/James_Wan "James Wan") said: "*Dead Silence*, my second movie and first studio film, was really me responding to the reaction that *[Saw](/wiki/Saw_(film) "Saw (film)")* was getting, meaning that people were harping on the torture aspect of *Saw*. So I made a very conscious decision to move away from that style of film and into something that was more of a haunted house, ghost story, which is a genre I love. But, of course, *Saw* made such a strong impression that it carried into *Dead Silence*. A lot of people were expecting something similar to *Saw*, but it wasn’t. Ultimately, it didn’t do that well, financially, and at the time, people didn’t really like it. But now, strangely, so many fans reach out to tell me how much they love *Dead Silence*."[[15]](#cite_note-15)
